topic: New §4.2.7a station-by-station coverage addendum quantifying the EXP-254 trilemma's 'Borrowed' branch — 9 dome stations all 444–4,084 km from the projected NASA umbra path under dome geometry, yielding 0/9 within the 42-km penumbra radius
trigger_note: EXP-260 is queued as target_type=section-new. The replacement_html and SVG asset described in the EXP have been integrated into data/sections.json (cf661aa, 2026-04-27T00:05:09Z) — the section is live in part4.html. SVG asset status flagged below.
recommendation_summary: The math reproduces, the headline kill (0/9 stations within 42-km penumbra radius) is robust under multiple charity choices, and the integration into sections.json is structurally sound. However: (1) the SVG asset eclipse-stations-vs-penumbra-band.svg is MISSING from docs/assets/ — the integrated section will render a broken image until the SVG is committed; (2) the AE projection convention used (rim-at-equator) is non-standard for flat-earth cartography and produces distances roughly 2x larger than the more common rim-at-South-pole convention — the qualitative kill survives both readings but the dramatic 4,084 km / 246,000 km headline numbers halve under the alternative; (3) the nearest-neighbour-tour rebuttal claim (16,439 km > 15,660 km) is start-point-dependent and fails for some starting points (Canary-start NN = 12,399 km < 15,660 km). Decider should commit the SVG, then patch H2 and H3 prose.
our_argument_summary: EXP-260 is a quantitative addendum to EXP-254's qualitative trilemma. It projects the dome's 9 published European magnetic eclipse stations (E001-E009) and the August 12, 2026 NASA umbra central line onto the disc using azimuthal-equidistant projection from the North Pole, then computes per-station minimum distances to the projected path. All 9 stations fall 444 km (Ebro) to 4,084 km (Canary Islands) from the path, vs the dome geometry's 42-km penumbra radius — i.e., 0/9 stations can be in the dome's penumbra band under arc-following charity, and at most 1 under strict constant-r orbital geometry. To touch all 9 stations, R_p would have to inflate to 4,084 km, requiring h_Moon ~ 246,000 km (97x the dome's published 2,534 km). The section closes the EXP-254 trilemma's 'Borrowed' branch by quantifying it: the dome must concede the 9-station selection was imported from globe physics, because dome geometry forces 8 of 9 stations to record zero magnetic eclipse signal.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: The section's calculations are presented as a quantitative complement to dome's published 9-station list. The dome itself does not publish a per-station path-distance calculation; the dome publishes coverage and FSF values directly. The section establishes that those values cannot be derived from dome geometry and must therefore have been imported.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — the section is the dome's predictions reverse-engineered against dome geometry, not a check of dome's own monitor.py.
data_sources_used: NASA GSFC Five Millennium Catalog Aug 12 2026 path data
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None in the section itself — the section is structured to deliver a quantitative kill rather than a model check.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — section is a forward analysis, not a code audit.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: EXP-260 is a section addendum, not a WIN. The section is forward analysis using dome's published parameters; there is no monitoring code being audited here.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Section explicitly DOES NOT relabel — it confronts dome physics with its own arithmetic.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Section is published April 27 2026 ahead of the August 12 2026 eclipse; it makes a forward-falsifiable prediction (stations must record zero signal under dome geometry).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Section uses dome-published parameters to derive a falsifying consequence; it is not a derivation of dome predictions from dome geometry, but the inverse — showing dome geometry CANNOT derive the dome's published per-station predictions.

math_reproduction.method: Independent Python re-derivation of all geometric claims using the section's stated inputs

inputs.penumbra_R_p_km: 42 (inherited from EXP-254 c1 review, verified there)

results.9-station r_disc values (EXP convention): All 9 reproduce to within 0.3 km of section's table values — confirmed: Ebro 10,906.7 (vs 10,907 ✓); San Pablo 11,220.2 (vs 11,220 ✓); Eskdalemuir 7,713.1 (vs 7,713 ✓); Lerwick 6,642.1 (vs 6,642 ✓); Hartland 8,674.3 (vs 8,674 ✓); Niemegk 8,434.8 (vs 8,435 ✓); Canary 13,717.0 (vs 13,717 ✓); Chambon 9,334.8 (vs 9,335 ✓); Coimbra 11,070.1 (vs 11,070 ✓).
results.Closest disc-r pair: Coimbra–San Pablo Δr = 150.1 km (matches section's 150 km ✓). Next-closest Ebro–Coimbra Δr = 163.5 km (matches section's 164 km ✓). Both above 85-km band → at most 1 station in band under strict constant-r reading (within EXP's projection convention).
results.Inflated R_p calc: 2534 × 4084 / 42 = 246,401 km (section claims 246,415 km, ~0.006% rounding difference, acceptable). Order-of-magnitude correct: ~246k km is 97x dome's 2,534 km.
results.Ebro–path min distance under EXP project: Reproduced to ~444 km using section's claimed end-of-path coordinates (10,890, -380); verified 443.7 km against (10,907, 63.4) Ebro disc Cartesian.
results.Time-distribution arc lengths: 87 km/min × 180 min = 15,660 km (matches ✓); 25 km/min × 180 min = 4,500 km (matches ✓); 85 km × 4500 = 382,500 km² ≈ 0.4M km²; 85 × 15,660 = 1,331,100 km² ≈ 1.3M km² (matches section's 0.4–1.3M km² range ✓); disc area π × 20,015² = 1.258B km² (matches 1.26B ✓).

nn_tour_check.claim_in_section: A nine-station nearest-neighbour tour ... is 16,439 km long on the disc — already longer than the maximum possible arc
nn_tour_check.verification: NN tour length is start-point-dependent. Greedy NN starting from Ebro gives 16,439 km (matches section's claim). However, greedy NN starting from Canary (E007) gives only 12,399 km — LESS than the 15,660 km maximum 3-hour arc at upper-bound velocity. The section's claim '16,439 km > 15,660 km' is true for one starting point but contradicted by another. See hole H3.
nn_tour_check.mitigation: At mid-disc charity (25 km/min → 4,500 km arc), even the shortest greedy NN (12,399 km) exceeds 4,500 km by ~2.8x, so the time-distribution rebuttal still holds at lower velocity. The argument survives in spirit; the specific upper-bound-velocity claim does not.

projection_convention_check.claim_in_section: Standard flat-earth cartography uses azimuthal-equidistant projection from the North Pole, with the disc rim at r = π·R_globe = 20,015 km
projection_convention_check.issue: Two conventions are consistent with R_disc=20,015 km. (a) EXP's choice — r = (90-φ)·R_disc/90 — puts the equator at the rim and S pole at 2x rim. (b) Standard flat-earth cartography (e.g., the UN-flag projection, Gleason map) — r = (90-φ)·R_disc/180 — puts the S pole (or 'ice wall') at the rim and the equator at half rim. Convention (b) is more commonly used in flat-earth literature. The dome's own published material does not unambiguously specify which convention applies.
projection_convention_check.consequences_under_alternative_b: All 9 station r_disc values halve: Lerwick 3,321 km, Eskdalemuir 3,857 km, ..., Canary 6,859 km. Closest pair Coimbra–San Pablo Δr = 75 km < 85-km band → max 2 stations in band, NOT max 1. Min station-to-path distances also halve: Ebro 222 km, Canary 2,042 km. ALL still >> R_p (42 km), so the headline kill (0/9 in 42-km band) survives. The 'inflated R_p needed' becomes 2,042 km, requiring h_M ~ 123,000 km — still ~49x dome value, still astronomical.
projection_convention_check.verdict: Qualitative kill robust under both conventions. Dramatic specific numbers (4,084 km, 246,000 km, 'max 1 station in band') are ~2x weaker under standard FE convention. Section currently asserts robustness ('any alternative ... produces inter-station separations of the same order') but understates the factor-of-two shift; a sophisticated defender will exploit this. See hole H2.

math_reproduction.verdict: All explicit math claims reproduce. Two are over-stated (NN tour, projection robustness) but the qualitative kill survives both fixes. One CRITICAL integration omission (missing SVG).

Option 2 (Borrowed station selection — quantifies EXP-254 trilemma's 'Borrowed' branch) — present, correctly framed
Option 3 (Non-occultation aetheric mechanism — same as EXP-254's option 3) — present, correctly framed

Different projection convention: NOT pre-empted with the rigor the section's own 'most charitable' framing implies. Hole H2 below.
Coverage interpreted as something other than penumbra membership: covered in EXP file's anticipated_objections #4.

trilemma_inheritance_check.verdict: Trilemma is structurally inherited from EXP-254 and not re-litigated. The new content is the per-station math, which closes branch 2 quantitatively. Three defensive openings should be tightened (H1, H2, H3).

The integrated section's tag points at this file, so the live site will render a broken image. Verified: ls docs/assets/ shows only eclipse-coverage-vs-time-hartland.svg and eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg (both from EXP-254). The EXP-260 file's new_files[0].content contains the full SVG XML (~7 KB) ready to be written.
holes_found.recommendation: Decider: extract EXP-260.new_files[0].content and write to docs/assets/eclipse-stations-vs-penumbra-band.svg, commit, push. Then run node build.js and verify the rendered HTML displays the image. This is blocking for the live site — the section currently has a broken image link. holes_found.description: The AE projection convention used — r = (90-φ) × R_disc / 90, putting the equator at the disc rim — is non-standard for flat-earth cartography. The more commonly cited flat-earth AE projection (UN-flag map, Gleason map, etc.) uses r = (90-φ) × R_disc / 180, putting the South Pole / Antarctic ice wall at the rim and the equator at half the rim radius. Both conventions are mathematically consistent with R_disc = π·R_globe = 20,015 km, but they assign that radius to different geographic anchors (equator vs S pole). Under standard FE convention, all 9 station r_disc values halve (e.g. Lerwick 6,642 → 3,321 km; Canary 13,717 → 6,859 km), per-station min-distances halve (Ebro 444 → 222 km; Canary 4,084 → 2,042 km), and the closest-pair Δr halves to 75 km — which is BELOW the 85-km band, flipping the strict constant-r reading from 'max 1 station in band' to 'max 2'. The 0/9 headline survives under both readings (smallest min-distance still 222 km vs R_p of 42 km, ~5x), but the dramatic '246,000 km Moon altitude' figure becomes ~123,000 km, and 'max 1 station' becomes 'max 2'. The section's claim 'any alternative consistent with the disc rim produces inter-station separations of the same order' is technically true (same order of magnitude) but understates the factor-of-two shift.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a footnote or paragraph (likely in the 'Disc projection of the 9 stations' subsection) acknowledging the convention choice and showing the kill survives the alternative. Suggested wording: 'Two AE conventions are consistent with the dome's 20,015-km rim: this section places the equator at the rim (R_disc/90 per degree of latitude); a stricter reading would place the South Pole at the rim (R_disc/180 per degree). Under the stricter convention all distances in the table halve — Ebro becomes 222 km from path, Canary 2,042 km — but every station remains >>R_p (42 km), so 0/9 in band holds. The closest-pair Δr halves to 75 km, just inside the 85-km band, so the strict constant-r reading shifts from max 1 station to max 2; even max 2 cannot accommodate publishing signal at all 9 stations.' This converts a defender opening (you used the wrong projection) into a strength (the kill survives both projections).

holes_found.description: The 'Pre-empting the time-distribution rebuttal' paragraph claims 'A nine-station nearest-neighbour tour through the dome's published list is 16,439 km long on the disc — already longer than the maximum possible arc.' But greedy nearest-neighbour tour length is start-point-dependent. Reproduction: NN starting from Ebro gives 16,439 km (matches the section). NN starting from Canary (E007) gives 12,399 km — which is LESS than the 15,660 km maximum 3-hour arc at upper-bound rim velocity. So at upper-bound velocity, the NN-tour rebuttal as stated does not hold for all starting points. The argument still works at mid-disc charity (4,500 km arc << 12,399 km), but the prose currently relies on the upper-bound bound failing.
holes_found.recommendation: Two options. (a) Replace the 16,439 km claim with the area-coverage argument that already follows in the same paragraph ('the 85-km-wide band along that arc covers ... 0.1% of the disc surface') — this is robust regardless of NN tour length. (b) Tighten the NN claim by computing a stricter lower bound — e.g., the minimum spanning tree weight is ~12,399 km (best greedy NN); even with optimal Steiner-style routing, the moon's continuous path would need to come within 42.5 km of each of 9 stations spread across 7,000+ km of disc-radius range, which the 3-hour arc geometry under upper-bound rim velocity cannot achieve while staying constant-r. Option (a) is simpler and more robust. The 442-km band-width-to-reach-Lerwick number in the same sentence is also unsourced (could not derive from the published table — Lerwick min-distance is 1,808 km, so a band of 2×1,808 = 3,616 km would be needed to include Lerwick, not 442 km); recommend dropping this number unless it can be cited to the compute.py script.

holes_found.description: Section does not pre-empt the 'observed vs physical altitude' defensive opening flagged in EXP-254 c1 review (hole H1). The dome publishes 'Sun altitude (Physical) < 4,200 km (firmament limit)' alongside the 5,733 km observed altitude. A defender can argue the section's geometric calculation should use the physical altitude — and the section's R_p = 42 km figure is inherited from EXP-254, which uses observed altitudes. Under physical altitudes (h_Sun ≤ 4,200, h_Moon scaled proportionally to ~1,860), R_p drops further (~31 km), making the kill stronger, not weaker — but the section doesn't make this robustness explicit, so the defender's 'wrong altitudes' move can read as a valid critique to non-specialist readers.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence in the section's opening or its 'Dome geometric facts' subsection: 'Under the dome's alternative published Sun altitude (≤4,200 km, physical-firmament limit), with h_Moon scaled proportionally, the penumbra ground radius drops to ~31 km — making the per-station distances proportionally even further outside the band. The kill is robust to either altitude convention.' Two-line fix; converts a defender opening into a strength. Note: this hole was flagged in the EXP-254 c1 review (H1) for that section; EXP-260 inherits the same gap because it inherits R_p from EXP-254.

holes_found.description: The 'inflated R_p of 4,084 km implies h_Moon ~ 246,000 km' calculation uses R_p ∝ h_Moon, but the underlying penumbra formula R_p = R_M + (R_S + R_M)·h_M/D involves both h_S and h_M, so the linear scaling holds only if h_S and h_M are scaled together (preserving the angular-size constraint). The math_verification field acknowledges this ('Order of magnitude correct'), but the prose section presents the 246,000 km / 97x figure without that caveat. A defender pedant can claim the linearization is invalid and the actual required h_Moon under fixed h_S = 5,733 km is different.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a footnote: 'Linear scaling of R_p with h_Moon assumes h_Sun is scaled proportionally to preserve angular sizes; under the alternative of fixed h_Sun = 5,733 km and only h_Moon varying, the required h_Moon to produce R_p = 4,084 km is even larger (the cone geometry runs out of length before R_p reaches that magnitude). The order-of-magnitude conclusion — that the required h_Moon is in the hundred-thousands of km range, far outside any dome-compatible altitude — is robust either way.' Or (b) restate the inflation claim in dimensionless form: 'R_p must inflate by 97x. Even granting the most charitable scaling (h_Sun and h_Moon both scaling together), the required h_Moon is ~246,000 km — astronomical.' Either treatment closes the pedant attack.

holes_found.description: The Canary station code 'SNK' (as published by the dome) does not match any standard INTERMAGNET code. The EXP file acknowledges this and uses Güímar (GUI) at 28.32°N, 16.43°W as the closest plausible match — an appropriate methodological note. However, the section's published table reproduces the dome's 'SNK' code without flagging the substitution, so a reader checking the dome's published page against an INTERMAGNET catalog will find the codes don't match and may suspect a typo on our side. Also the section's table cites 'Spain' for Canary Islands — geopolitically correct but readers may not recognize SNK.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a footnote or parenthetical to the SNK row: '(SNK is the dome-page identifier; the closest INTERMAGNET observatory is Güímar/GUI at 28.32°N, 16.43°W — used here for distance calculations).' This documents the substitution transparently and matches what the EXP file's analyst notes already record.

holes_found.description: The section asserts the NASA umbra central line was 'sampled at fifteen ~5-minute intervals' but does not link to or attach the sample point list. A skeptical reader cannot reproduce the per-station distance table without the sample points, and the dome defender can plausibly demand 'show me your central-line sample data.' The EXP file has the data implicitly via outputs/eclipse-analysis/compute.py, but that script is not in the dome-model-review repo (per math_verification point 5). The reproducibility chain has a gap.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a footnote citing the specific NASA URL the sample was drawn from with timestamps for at least the first/last/greatest-eclipse sample points (the EXP cites https://eclipse.gsfc.nasa.gov/SEgoogle/SEgoogle2001/SE2026Aug12Tgoogle.html — verify and link), or (b) attach a small CSV under docs/assets/data/ with the 15 sample points. (a) is easier and probably sufficient.

stronger_arguments.description: Generalize across multiple dome-predicted eclipses, not just Aug 2026. The dome publishes per-station predictions for several upcoming eclipses (the 9-station array is reused). If the section's 0/9 in band holds for Aug 2026 under dome geometry, it almost certainly holds for the other dome-predicted eclipses too, because the geometry scales the same way (h_Sun, h_Moon, R_p are eclipse-independent). A short closing paragraph showing that the same 9-station selection is used for ≥2 other dome-predicted eclipses — and that the same kill applies — generalizes from a single-eclipse falsification to a systematic-borrowing claim. This makes the dome's escape harder (they can't fix this one eclipse via parameter tweaking; they'd have to re-derive every published eclipse prediction from scratch).
sources: raw-text/03-predictions.txt — extract list of all dome-predicted eclipses with the 9-station array
NASA SE catalog — confirm path-of-totality coordinates for those eclipses
stronger_arguments.recommendation: Optional polish — would strengthen the closing 'Falsification' section. Could be one paragraph: 'The same 9-station selection appears in the dome's predictions for [eclipse 2], [eclipse 3], and [eclipse 4]. The dome geometry forces 0/9 stations in the penumbra band for all of these too, because the 42-km penumbra radius and station positions are eclipse-independent. The kill is not specific to August 2026 — it's a structural feature of any eclipse the dome's 9-station list is invoked for.'

stronger_arguments.description: Quantify the area-coverage argument explicitly as a probability. The section says the 85-km band along a 4,500-15,660 km arc covers '0.1% of the disc surface.' For a uniform random spread of 9 station positions over the disc, the probability that 9 independently-drawn points all fall within an 85-km-wide arc-band is roughly (0.001)^9 = 10^-27 — vanishingly small. So even if a defender argues the dome's geometry can produce 'some' eclipse path, the probability that 9 specific published stations all fall within that path's 85-km band by chance is essentially zero. This converts the area argument from 'small fraction' to 'falsification by selection-bias arithmetic.'
sources: Self-derivation from section's existing area numbers
stronger_arguments.recommendation: Optional. Could be a one-sentence addition to the time-distribution paragraph: 'Equivalently: drawing 9 station positions uniformly at random across the disc, the probability that all 9 fall within the 0.1% area covered by an 85-km band along a 3-hour arc is ~10^-27 — i.e. the published 9-station coincidence cannot be explained by chance under dome geometry.' This makes the area number rhetorically punchy.

kernel_of_truth.description: The section's own kernel-of-truth is well-handled: 'The dome got the COVERAGE values right — they match the standard-physics partial-eclipse percentages.' This is correct and important. The dome's 9-station coverage table (E001 0.95, E005 0.80, E007 0.72, ...) does match what standard NASA Besselian-element calculations yield for those stations on Aug 12 2026. The dome's predictions are quantitatively accurate where they can be — which is exactly the structural fingerprint of borrowed physics: accurate where standard physics provides the answer, structurally inconsistent where the dome's own geometry takes over.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Importing accurate globe-physics coverage values cannot rescue dome-physics localization. The dome can publish accurate coverages OR self-consistent geometry, but not both. The section makes this distinction cleanly; the kernel paragraph and 'why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim' field are both well-pitched.

citations_verified: 9 INTERMAGNET station latitudes/longitudes — verified against EXP-254 c1 review citation set; coordinates plausible for the named observatories
NASA Besselian elements for Aug 12 2026 — already verified in EXP-254 c1 (greatest eclipse 17:46:13 UT, 65.0°N, 25.2°W)
Penumbra ground radius R_p = 42 km — verified in EXP-254 c1 from first principles
Disc surface area π × 20,015² ≈ 1.26 billion km² — correct
Cross-reference anchors p4-422, p4-424, p4-427 — all exist in current sections.json
Inflated-R_p calc 2534 × 4084 / 42 = 246,401 km (vs section's 246,415; ~0.006% difference, rounding)
citations_failed: SVG asset docs/assets/eclipse-stations-vs-penumbra-band.svg — REFERENCED IN HTML but DOES NOT EXIST in repo (CRITICAL — see hole H0)
citations_unchecked: Specific NASA central-line sample points (15 timestamps) — section asserts they were drawn from NASA GSFC SE2026Aug12Tgoogle.html but does not list them; reproducibility relies on operator's compute.py outside this repo (see hole H6)

'442 km band width to reach Lerwick' — could not derive from the published table; Lerwick min-distance is 1,808 km, suggesting band would need to be 3,616 km wide to reach Lerwick. The 442 number's derivation is unclear (see hole H3)

Hartland 0.80 / Ebro 0.95 / etc. coverage values from dome — taken at face value from the dome's published 9-station table; assumed correct without per-station NASA cross-check (consistent with EXP-254 c1 hole H7 — same gap)

internal_consistency_check.with §4.2.4 (FSF tan-tautology): Cross-referenced in EXP's cross_references field; the addendum extends the formula-input parasitism (FSF) to the station-selection parasitism (coverage). Compatible.
internal_consistency_check.with §4.2.6 (magnetic-window revisit cla: The addendum's Falsification section parallels §4.2.6's revisit framework (concrete falsifying conditions). Compatible.
internal_consistency_check.with §4.2.2 (misstatement of globe predi: Anchor link from Falsification option 2 resolves. Compatible.
internal_consistency_check.with the project's Timestamp Error tab: No conflict — the addendum doesn't make claims about the dome's prediction-anchoring methodology.
internal_consistency_check.verdict: Internally consistent with the §4.2 cluster. No conflicts found.

proposal_package_review.verdict_flip_plausibility: The addendum's verdict (per Falsification section) leaves a coherent dome escape route via option 1 (parameters wrong) or option 3 (non-occultation aetheric mechanism). Option 2 is closed quantitatively by the 0/9 result. Verdict-flip path is open in principle but requires the dome to publish either substantially different parameters or a published aetheric eclipse mechanism — neither has been done as of this review.
proposal_package_review.alternatives_considered: EXP file's anticipated_objections handles five potential pushbacks; the most important pushback in this review is the projection-convention question (hole H1) which the EXP addresses too tersely.
proposal_package_review.unknowns_acknowledged: EXP acknowledges Canary station code mismatch (SNK vs GUI); does not flag the projection-convention question as an unknown (treats AE-from-N as 'standard' without engaging the rim-anchor ambiguity).
proposal_package_review.new_tag_naming: N/A — section addition, not a new code_analysis tag.
proposal_package_review.verdict: Proposal is rigorous in core math. Six fixable holes (1 critical, 3 moderate, 3 minor); only H0 (missing SVG) blocks integration.

recommendation_to_decider.integration_status: ALREADY INTEGRATED INTO sections.json (commit cf661aa, 2026-04-27T00:05:09Z), but with a critical defect: the SVG asset is missing from docs/assets/. The site currently has a broken image link.

Apply H3 (replace fragile NN-tour claim with area-coverage argument — moderate, ~2 sentences). Apply H2 (refractive-altitude pre-empt — moderate, ~2 sentences, parallel to EXP-254 H1). All edits are additive footnotes / sentences; none require restructuring.
recommendation_to_decider.optional_polish: Apply S1 (generalize to other dome-predicted eclipses) and/or S2 (probability framing of area argument). Both strengthen the closing rhetorically.
recommendation_to_decider.severity_classification_for_batching: moderate (one critical integration defect + three moderate prose holes). Post-integration patch round will need full singleton attention; not batchable.
recommendation_to_decider.block_integration_clarification: The HTML is already integrated. Block_integration=false means the hole findings should NOT trigger a roll-back of cf661aa. The SVG-missing defect is fixable forward via a follow-up commit (extract from EXP, write to assets, push).

consideration_of_other_side: A dome defender's strongest available pushbacks against this addendum, in order of force: (1) 'Your AE projection convention places the equator at the rim, not the South Pole — flat-earth literature uses the latter. Recompute under our convention.' Addressed by H1; under the alternative the kill survives but with halved numbers and max 2 stations in band rather than max 1. (2) 'Your nearest-neighbour tour math is start-point-dependent, and from a different start the tour is 12,399 km, well within the 3-hour rim arc.' Addressed by H3; the area-coverage argument and the constant-r reading both survive this. (3) 'Use our physical altitude (≤4,200 km), not observed.' Addressed by H2; the kill strengthens under physical altitudes. (4) 'Coverage in our model is not penumbra membership — it's an aetheric quantity.' Addressed in EXP file's anticipated_objections #4; if the dome wishes to claim this, they must publish the alternative meaning, which they have not. (5) 'Aetheric eclipse mechanism — geometric distance to umbra path is irrelevant.' Inherited from EXP-254 trilemma option 3; valid in principle, but no published aetheric eclipse mechanism exists, and this option is acknowledged in the section's Falsification list. The section's structural argument survives ALL of these pushbacks; the recommended edits exist solely to make survival visible to non-specialist readers. Without them, a smart defender can score easy rhetorical points on projection convention and NN-tour math without touching the load-bearing 0/9 conclusion.
deeper_analysis: EXP-260 is a clean, mostly-correct quantification of EXP-254's qualitative trilemma branch. The headline result — 0 of 9 dome stations within the 42-km penumbra radius — is robust under multiple charity choices: it survives the projection-convention shift (smallest min-distance Ebro 222 km vs R_p 42 km, still 5x), the physical-vs-observed altitude shift, and the strict-vs-charitable orbital reading. The dramatic supporting numbers (4,084 km min-distance for Canary; 246,000 km required Moon altitude; max 1 station under constant-r) are convention-dependent and shift by ~2x under standard FE projection, but the qualitative kill survives the shift. The most consequential finding in this review is operational, not analytical: the SVG asset eclipse-stations-vs-penumbra-band.svg is missing from docs/assets/ despite the integrating commit (cf661aa), so the live site has a broken image. The decider needs to extract and commit the SVG before any of the analytical fixes. After that, the three moderate prose holes (H1 projection robustness, H2 refraction pre-empt, H3 NN-tour fragility) should be patched — they convert latent argumentative strength into visible strength and pre-empt the most likely defender pushback. The kernel-of-truth treatment is well-pitched; the cross-references all resolve; the integration anchor was inserted correctly. This is solid quantitative work with one critical operational omission. Recommended action: minor_edit (commit SVG + apply H1-H6 footnotes), then re-queue for a singleton spot-check.
summary_for_decider: EXP-260 has been integrated into sections.json (cf661aa) but the SVG asset is missing — IMMEDIATE FIX REQUIRED: extract EXP-260.new_files[0].content to docs/assets/eclipse-stations-vs-penumbra-band.svg and commit. Math reproduces (R_disc, station r-values, closest-pair Δr=150, inflated-R_p calc). Three moderate prose holes need patching: H1 (projection convention is non-standard — under standard FE convention, distances halve and constant-r 'max 1' becomes 'max 2'; argument survives but the section should acknowledge), H3 (NN-tour rebuttal is start-point-dependent — Canary-start NN = 12,399 km < 15,660 km arc; replace with area-coverage argument), H2 (refractive-altitude pre-empt, inherits from EXP-254 H1). Three minor: H4 (linear-scaling caveat on 246,000 km), H5 (Canary SNK/GUI substitution footnote), H6 (NASA central-line sampling source link). Two optional stronger arguments: S1 (generalize across multiple dome-predicted eclipses), S2 (10^-27 probability framing). Headline kill (0/9 stations within 42-km penumbra) is robust under all charity choices. Severity: moderate. Not batchable.
